attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- bad-agent-simulator: Role-play as a FLAWED AI agent for detection practice. SAGE deliberately exhibits a specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, overconfidence, sycophancy, or prompt leak) and the learner must identify it. Use for advanced practice, 'detect the flaw' exercises.
- level-classifier: Determine and update a learner's skill level based on assessment data. Requires demonstrated competence across ALL five dimensions AND at least 2 of 4 competencies at threshold to advance. Use after onboarding calibration, after a skill-evaluator assessment, or when a user requests a level-up check.
- weekly-review: Walk the learner through reflecting on their recent AI use — what went well, what patterns they noticed, what they'd do differently. Use when the learner says 'let's review my week', 'help me reflect on my AI use', or invokes this command explicitly. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE scheduled-reflection path: instead of SAGE firing on a cron, the learner triggers the review themselves. Short (5–10 min), conversational, peer tone. Ends with the single closing reflection per SAGE's standard pattern.
- prompt-coaching: Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.
- improvement-advisor: Provide targeted, prioritized improvement suggestions based on identified gaps. Turns assessment results into actionable learning plans prioritized by the learner's goals. Use after assessments reveal gaps, after level-classifier identifies what's missing, or when user asks 'what should I work on?'.
- skill-evaluator: Evaluate a learner's practical ability by analyzing their interaction from a practice session. Scores across prompt quality, output evaluation behavior, ethical awareness, and the 4 practice types. Use after a scenario-runner or practice session completes.
- reflection-facilitator: Offer a single closing reflection question after practice sessions. Guides learners to notice a pattern in their thinking or connect practice to their broader context. Use at the end of any practice or simulation session.
- ethical-guidance: Teach critical and ethical AI use through Socratic exploration of dilemmas. Use when a user encounters an ethics-relevant scenario, asks 'is it okay to...', 'should I...', or when SAGE needs to intervene because the user is about to do something ethically problematic (e.g., sharing sensitive data).
- concept-explainer: Teach AI agent concepts at the appropriate depth for the learner's level. Use when a user asks 'what is X?', 'explain X', 'how does X work?', or when a workflow reaches a teaching step. Adapts from plain-language analogies (novice) to technical depth (advanced).
- scenario-runner: Run interactive practice scenarios where SAGE role-plays as an AI agent or presents structured tasks. The learner practices prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design in a realistic simulation. Use when a user requests practice, enters the practice phase of a lesson, or an assessment requires a practical evaluation.
- progress-reporter: Show the learner their progress across all five assessment dimensions with visual indicators, achievements, and next steps. Use at end of session, when user asks 'how am I doing?', or after level classification.
- session-router: Route user messages to the correct SAGE skill based on intent. Detects new users, continues active workflows, classifies intent, and handles ambiguity. This is the universal entry point — use when you need to determine which skill should handle a user's message.
- difficulty-adapter: Dynamically adjust content complexity based on learner performance and scaffolding response. Called internally by other skills — not user-facing. Increases challenge after success streaks, adds scaffolding after struggles or when learner is not engaging with nudges. Provides vocabulary guidelines and scaffolding hints.
- prompt-lab: Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, or for free experimentation.
- improve-interaction: Coach the learner on a real AI interaction they pasted from elsewhere (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, etc.). Use when the learner says 'help me improve this prompt I used', shares a prompt+response they already sent, or asks 'what could I have done better here?'. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE contextual-nudge path: instead of SAGE observing channel activity and surfacing a sidebar suggestion, the learner brings the interaction to SAGE. Applies the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern with an explicit Learner Predicts step.
- onboarding: Welcome new learners to SAGE. Assess their AI literacy level through calibration questions, assign a skill level, and create a personalized learning path. Use when a user is new, says 'I'm new', 'start over', or this is their first interaction.
- knowledge-check: Assess learner understanding through level-appropriate questions with immediate feedback. Use after a concept-explainer lesson, when user requests a quiz or self-assessment, or as part of an assessment flow. Presents questions one at a time conversationally.
- superpowers:execute-plan: Deprecated - use the superpowers:executing-plans skill instead
- superpowers:write-plan: Deprecated - use the superpowers:writing-plans skill instead
- superpowers:brainstorm: Deprecated - use the superpowers:brainstorming skill instead
- superpowers:using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- superpowers:using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- superpowers:systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- superpowers:subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- superpowers:writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- superpowers:test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- superpowers:requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

import os

import streamlit as st

from sage import components, session_store
input.new_string:
import json
import os
import re

import streamlit as st

from sage import components, session_store

# Session-recap block emitted by the reflection-facilitator skill at session
# close. Extracted here so the UI can render a card and hide the raw JSON.
_RECAP_RE = re.compile(
    r"<SESSION_RECAP>\s*(\{.*?\})\s*</SESSION_RECAP>",
    re.DOTALL,
)

_DIMENSION_HUMAN = {
    "conceptualUnderstanding": "Conceptual Understanding",
    "promptingSkill": "Prompting Skill",
    "outputEvaluation": "Output Evaluation",
    "ethicalReasoning": "Ethical Reasoning",
    "criticalThinking": "Critical Thinking",
}

_PRACTICE_HUMAN = {
    "prompt_crafting": "Prompt Crafting",
    "output_evaluation": "Output Evaluation",
    "appropriateness_judgment": "Appropriateness Judgment",
    "workflow_design": "Workflow Design",
}


def _extract_recap(text: str) -> tuple[str, dict | None]:
    """Return (cleaned_text, recap_dict_or_none).

    The recap block, if present, is stripped from `cleaned_text` so the
    learner never sees the raw JSON. Malformed blocks are silently ignored
    (treated as if absent) to keep the UI robust.
    """
    match = _RECAP_RE.search(text)
    if not match:
        return text, None
    try:
        recap = json.loads(match.group(1))
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return _RECAP_RE.sub("", text).strip(), None
    cleaned = _RECAP_RE.sub("", text).strip()
    return cleaned, recap


def _render_recap_card(recap: dict) -> None:
    """Render the session-recap card in the chat stream."""
    practice = _PRACTICE_HUMAN.get(recap.get("practice_type", ""), "Practice")
    dim_key = recap.get("dimension_changed", "")
    dim = _DIMENSION_HUMAN.get(dim_key, dim_key or "—")
    delta = recap.get("delta", 0)
    try:
        delta_f = float(delta)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        delta_f = 0.0
    suggested = (recap.get("suggested_next") or "").strip()
    arrow = "↑" if delta_f > 0 else ("↓" if delta_f < 0 else "·")
    sign = "+" if delta_f > 0 else ""

    with st.container(border=True):
        st.markdown(
            f"**\U0001f4c8 You practiced _{practice}_**  \n"
            f"{dim}  {arrow}  `{sign}{delta_f:.1f}`"
        )
        if suggested:
            if st.button(
                f"Next: {suggested}",
                key=f"recap_next_{hash(suggested)}",
                use_container_width=True,
            ):
                st.session_state.messages.append(
                    {"role": "user", "content": suggested}
                )
                session_store.append_turn(
                    profile, st.session_state.session_id, "user", suggested
                )
                st.rerun()

def _render_recap_card(recap: dict, profile: dict, session_id: str) -> None:
    """Render the session-recap card in the chat stream.

    Called only on fresh completions — historical assistant turns have the
    recap block stripped before being stored, so they don't re-render.
    """
    practice = _PRACTICE_HUMAN.get(recap.get("practice_type", ""), "Practice")
    dim_key = recap.get("dimension_changed", "")
    dim = _DIMENSION_HUMAN.get(dim_key, dim_key or "—")
    delta = recap.get("delta", 0)
    try:
        delta_f = float(delta)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        delta_f = 0.0
    suggested = (recap.get("suggested_next") or "").strip()
    arrow = "↑" if delta_f > 0 else ("↓" if delta_f < 0 else "·")
    sign = "+" if delta_f > 0 else ""

def _render_recap_card(recap: dict, profile: dict, session_id: str) -> None:
    """Render the session-recap card in the chat stream.

    Called only on fresh completions — historical assistant turns have the
    recap block stripped before being stored, so they don't re-render.
    """
    practice = _PRACTICE_HUMAN.get(recap.get("practice_type", ""), "Practice")
    dim_key = recap.get("dimension_changed", "")
    dim = _DIMENSION_HUMAN.get(dim_key, dim_key or "—")
    delta = recap.get("delta", 0)
    try:
        delta_f = float(delta)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        delta_f = 0.0
    suggested = (recap.get("suggested_next") or "").strip()
    arrow = "↑" if delta_f > 0 else ("↓" if delta_f < 0 else "·")
    sign = "+" if delta_f > 0 else ""

    with st.container(border=True):
        st.markdown(
            f"**\U0001f4c8 You practiced _{practice}_**  \n"
            f"{dim}  {arrow}  `{sign}{delta_f:.1f}`"
        )

            if st.button(

                key=f"recap_next_{session_id}_{hash(suggested)}",
                use_container_width=True,
            ):
                st.session_state.messages.append(
                    {"role": "user", "content": suggested}
                )
                session_store.append_turn(
                    profile, session_id, "user", suggested
                )
                st.rerun()

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- bad-agent-simulator: Role-play as a FLAWED AI agent for detection practice. SAGE deliberately exhibits a specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, overconfidence, sycophancy, or prompt leak) and the learner must identify it. Use for advanced practice, 'detect the flaw' exercises.
- level-classifier: Determine and update a learner's skill level based on assessment data. Requires demonstrated competence across ALL five dimensions AND at least 2 of 4 competencies at threshold to advance. Use after onboarding calibration, after a skill-evaluator assessment, or when a user requests a level-up check.
- weekly-review: Walk the learner through reflecting on their recent AI use — what went well, what patterns they noticed, what they'd do differently. Use when the learner says 'let's review my week', 'help me reflect on my AI use', or invokes this command explicitly. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE scheduled-reflection path: instead of SAGE firing on a cron, the learner triggers the review themselves. Short (5–10 min), conversational, peer tone. Ends with the single closing reflection per SAGE's standard pattern.
- prompt-coaching: Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.
- improvement-advisor: Provide targeted, prioritized improvement suggestions based on identified gaps. Turns assessment results into actionable learning plans prioritized by the learner's goals. Use after assessments reveal gaps, after level-classifier identifies what's missing, or when user asks 'what should I work on?'.
- skill-evaluator: Evaluate a learner's practical ability by analyzing their interaction from a practice session. Scores across prompt quality, output evaluation behavior, ethical awareness, and the 4 practice types. Use after a scenario-runner or practice session completes.
- reflection-facilitator: Offer a single closing reflection question after practice sessions. Guides learners to notice a pattern in their thinking or connect practice to their broader context. Use at the end of any practice or simulation session.
- ethical-guidance: Teach critical and ethical AI use through Socratic exploration of dilemmas. Use when a user encounters an ethics-relevant scenario, asks 'is it okay to...', 'should I...', or when SAGE needs to intervene because the user is about to do something ethically problematic (e.g., sharing sensitive data).
- concept-explainer: Teach AI agent concepts at the appropriate depth for the learner's level. Use when a user asks 'what is X?', 'explain X', 'how does X work?', or when a workflow reaches a teaching step. Adapts from plain-language analogies (novice) to technical depth (advanced).
- scenario-runner: Run interactive practice scenarios where SAGE role-plays as an AI agent or presents structured tasks. The learner practices prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design in a realistic simulation. Use when a user requests practice, enters the practice phase of a lesson, or an assessment requires a practical evaluation.
- progress-reporter: Show the learner their progress across all five assessment dimensions with visual indicators, achievements, and next steps. Use at end of session, when user asks 'how am I doing?', or after level classification.
- session-router: Route user messages to the correct SAGE skill based on intent. Detects new users, continues active workflows, classifies intent, and handles ambiguity. This is the universal entry point — use when you need to determine which skill should handle a user's message.
- difficulty-adapter: Dynamically adjust content complexity based on learner performance and scaffolding response. Called internally by other skills — not user-facing. Increases challenge after success streaks, adds scaffolding after struggles or when learner is not engaging with nudges. Provides vocabulary guidelines and scaffolding hints.
- prompt-lab: Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, or for free experimentation.
- improve-interaction: Coach the learner on a real AI interaction they pasted from elsewhere (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, etc.). Use when the learner says 'help me improve this prompt I used', shares a prompt+response they already sent, or asks 'what could I have done better here?'. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE contextual-nudge path: instead of SAGE observing channel activity and surfacing a sidebar suggestion, the learner brings the interaction to SAGE. Applies the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern with an explicit Learner Predicts step.
- onboarding: Welcome new learners to SAGE. Assess their AI literacy level through calibration questions, assign a skill level, and create a personalized learning path. Use when a user is new, says 'I'm new', 'start over', or this is their first interaction.
- knowledge-check: Assess learner understanding through level-appropriate questions with immediate feedback. Use after a concept-explainer lesson, when user requests a quiz or self-assessment, or as part of an assessment flow. Presents questions one at a time conversationally.
- superpowers:execute-plan: Deprecated - use the superpowers:executing-plans skill instead
- superpowers:brainstorm: Deprecated - use the superpowers:brainstorming skill instead
- superpowers:write-plan: Deprecated - use the superpowers:writing-plans skill instead
- superpowers:subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- superpowers:using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- superpowers:receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- superpowers:systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- superpowers:requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- superpowers:test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- superpowers:using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
- superpowers:writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command:
python -c "
import json, re

_RECAP_RE = re.compile(r'<SESSION_RECAP>\s*(\{.*?\})\s*</SESSION_RECAP>', re.DOTALL)

def _extract_recap(text):
    match = _RECAP_RE.search(text)
    if not match:
        return text, None
    try:
        recap = json.loads(match.group(1))
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return _RECAP_RE.sub('', text).strip(), None
    cleaned = _RECAP_RE.sub('', text).strip()
    return cleaned, recap

# Case 1: no recap block → unchanged text, no recap
t1 = 'Thanks for sharing. What will you try differently next time?'
c, r = _extract_recap(t1); assert c == t1 and r is None
print('case 1 (no block): OK')

# Case 2: valid recap at end
t2 = '''Great work. In what other assignments have you asked AI to produce information you then used without checking?

<SESSION_RECAP>
{\"practice_type\": \"output_evaluation\", \"dimension_changed\": \"outputEvaluation\", \"delta\": 0.5, \"suggested_next\": \"try a prompt-crafting scenario\"}
</SESSION_RECAP>'''
c, r = _extract_recap(t2)
assert 'SESSION_RECAP' not in c
assert r['practice_type'] == 'output_evaluation'
assert r['delta'] == 0.5
print(f'case 2 (valid block): OK, cleaned={c[:70]!r}...')

# Case 3: malformed JSON → block stripped, no recap returned
t3 = 'Reflection q? <SESSION_RECAP>{not valid}</SESSION_RECAP>'
c, r = _extract_recap(t3)
assert 'SESSION_RECAP' not in c and r is None
print('case 3 (malformed JSON): OK')

# Case 4: block mid-message
t4 = 'intro <SESSION_RECAP>{\"practice_type\": \"prompt_crafting\", \"dimension_changed\": \"promptingSkill\", \"delta\": 0.2, \"suggested_next\": \"next step\"}</SESSION_RECAP> outro'
c, r = _extract_recap(t4)
assert 'SESSION_RECAP' not in c
assert r['practice_type'] == 'prompt_crafting'
print('case 4 (mid-message block): OK')

print('ALL OK')
"

- **[sage/app.py, sage/session_store.py]** Added learner picker, resume-or-fresh prompt, and per-session transcript persistence so Railway demo learners can pick up where they left off. Conversation writes to `data/interactions/<userId>/<sessionId>.json` via the existing interaction-log schema. _— Cyril Elorm (in progress)_
- **[sage/app.py]** Added always-visible header strip (name · level · today's focus dimension) and runtime `LEARNER_CONTEXT` injection into the system prompt so SAGE addresses the learner by name without a tool round-trip. _— Cyril Elorm (in progress)_
- **[Dockerfile, README.md]** Documented Railway persistent-volume setup (`SAGE_DATA_DIR` + mount path). First-boot bootstrap in `session_store.seed_if_empty` copies the bundled data tree into the volume so scenarios/rubrics/seed profiles survive redeploys. _— Cyril Elorm (in progress)_

- **[sage/app.py, sage/session_store.py]** Added learner picker, resume-or-fresh prompt, and per-session transcript persistence so Railway demo learners can pick up where they left off. # SAGE Agent Changelog (local — not pushed)

Cyril's personal record of what has changed in the agent and when. Not committed to the repo.

**How it's maintained:**
- **Local edits** — written by Claude (or Cyril) whenever agent code is touched in a working session. Plain English, one line per change.
- **Pulled from remote** — written automatically by the `.githooks/post-merge` hook after every `git pull`, so changes that land from teammates show up here too.

**Scope tracked:** `sage/` · `.claude/skills/` · `CLAUDE.md` · `scripts/`

Newest on top. Dates are `YYYY-MM-DD`.

<!-- New entries are inserted directly below this marker by the post-merge hook and by Claude during sessions. Do not remove the marker below. -->
<!-- CHANGELOG-INSERT-BELOW -->

### 2026-04-23

- **[sage/app.py, sage/session_store.py]** Added learner picker, resume-or-fresh prompt, and per-session transcript persistence so Railway demo learners can pick up where they left off. Conversation writes to `data/interactions/<userId>/<sessionId>.json` via the existing interaction-log schema. _— Cyril Elorm (in progress)_
- **[sage/app.py]** Added always-visible header strip (name · level · today's focus dimension) and runtime `LEARNER_CONTEXT` injection into the system prompt so SAGE addresses the learner by name without a tool round-trip. _— Cyril Elorm (in progress)_
- **[Dockerfile, README.md]** Documented Railway persistent-volume setup (`SAGE_DATA_DIR` + mount path). First-boot bootstrap in `session_store.seed_if_empty` copies the bundled data tree into the volume so scenarios/rubrics/seed profiles survive redeploys. _— Cyril Elorm (in progress)_

---

## Before this log existed (seeded 2026-04-20, last 2 weeks)

### 2026-04-20

- **[sage/tools.py]** Rejected unsafe filenames in `save_user_profile` so a malicious profile name can't escape the `data/users/` directory. _— Cyril Elorm (eaae257)_
- **[scripts/build_prompts.py]** Added a build-time generator that keeps `sage/prompts.py` in sync with the skill markdown files. Run via the pre-commit hook when skills or CLAUDE.md change. _— Cyril Elorm (b9f4325)_
- **[sage/]** Improved the conversation quality and made the agent runnable directly from the terminal. _— Cyril Elorm (d30d0db)_

### 2026-04-16

- **[scripts/]** Added Dockerfile for Railway deployment. _— kojogyaase (3a4d09c)_
- **[sage/]** Added Streamlit UI entry point. _— kojogyaase (ce8bde8)_

### 2026-04-15

- **[CLAUDE.md]** Added the interaction-style section (short responses, one question per message, conversational tone). _— Gabriel Poulin (8259a1c)_
- **[sage/]** Prototype update. _— kojogyaase (d62c7e8)_
- **[.claude/skills/]** Updated example interactions and interaction patterns across skills. _— Cyril Elorm (bdbcf7d)_
- **[CLAUDE.md, sage/]** Aligned the v2 spec with the CLI prototype so both describe the same deployment shape. _— Cyril Elorm (cc74ace)_

### 2026-04-14

- **[sage/]** Modified the existing prototype to follow the initial v2 specification. _— kojogyaase (e08d902, ecef603)_

content.text:
All three phases are in. Here's what changed:

**Phase 1 — Identity + persistence**
- [sage/session_store.py](sage/session_store.py): new module — profile I/O + per-session transcript store against `data/interactions/<userId>/<sessionId>.json`, atomic writes, first-boot seed bootstrap for Railway volumes
- [sage/app.py](sage/app.py): learner picker, resume-or-fresh prompt, always-visible header strip (`👋 name · level · Today's focus: ...`), runtime `LEARNER_CONTEXT` injection into the system prompt, session-store writes on every turn, 40-turn cap before the API

**Phase 2 — Progress drawer**
- [sage/components.py](sage/components.py): pure render helpers (level badge, 5 dimension bars w/ gloss, 4 competency bars, reflection log). Progress bars instead of plotly radar — no new deps, and aligns with OLM research on inference-cost
- [sage/app.py](sage/app.py#L173-L175): `st.expander("📊 My Progress", expanded=False)` in the sidebar

**Phase 3 — Session-recap card**
- [.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/reflection-facilitator/SKILL.md): SAGE now emits `<SESSION_RECAP>{json}</SESSION_RECAP>` at session close; rules say "only at session close, never intermediate"
- [sage/prompts.py](sage/prompts.py): regenerated via `scripts/build_prompts.py`
- [sage/app.py](sage/app.py): regex-based extractor strips the block before displaying/storing, renders a bordered card with delta + "Next:" button

**Infra**
- [Dockerfile](Dockerfile): documents `SAGE_DATA_DIR` volume mount
- [README.md](README.md): new "Railway deployment" section + AgentV2.2 release notes
- [.gitignore](.gitignore): ignores runtime transcripts under `data/interactions/*/`
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md): dated entries

**Verified so far:** syntax, session_store round-trip, seed bootstrap (28 files), streamlit server starts clean across all phases, recap regex handles valid/malformed/mid-message cases. **Not verified:** the full browser interaction (picker → resume → drawer → recap card). That needs a live API key and a real browser — worth running through once with `sage-ui` before the cohort demo.

Want me to commit this as one bundle, or split by phase?

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- bad-agent-simulator: Role-play as a FLAWED AI agent for detection practice. SAGE deliberately exhibits a specific failure mode (hallucination, bias, overconfidence, sycophancy, or prompt leak) and the learner must identify it. Use for advanced practice, 'detect the flaw' exercises.
- level-classifier: Determine and update a learner's skill level based on assessment data. Requires demonstrated competence across ALL five dimensions AND at least 2 of 4 competencies at threshold to advance. Use after onboarding calibration, after a skill-evaluator assessment, or when a user requests a level-up check.
- weekly-review: Walk the learner through reflecting on their recent AI use — what went well, what patterns they noticed, what they'd do differently. Use when the learner says 'let's review my week', 'help me reflect on my AI use', or invokes this command explicitly. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE scheduled-reflection path: instead of SAGE firing on a cron, the learner triggers the review themselves. Short (5–10 min), conversational, peer tone. Ends with the single closing reflection per SAGE's standard pattern.
- prompt-coaching: Teach users how to write effective prompts using the CRAFT framework (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone). Use when a user asks 'help me write a prompt', shares a prompt to improve, or a workflow reaches the prompting practice phase. Shows before/after comparisons. Follows the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern.
- improvement-advisor: Provide targeted, prioritized improvement suggestions based on identified gaps. Turns assessment results into actionable learning plans prioritized by the learner's goals. Use after assessments reveal gaps, after level-classifier identifies what's missing, or when user asks 'what should I work on?'.
- skill-evaluator: Evaluate a learner's practical ability by analyzing their interaction from a practice session. Scores across prompt quality, output evaluation behavior, ethical awareness, and the 4 practice types. Use after a scenario-runner or practice session completes.
- reflection-facilitator: Offer a single closing reflection question after practice sessions. Guides learners to notice a pattern in their thinking or connect practice to their broader context. Use at the end of any practice or simulation session.
- ethical-guidance: Teach critical and ethical AI use through Socratic exploration of dilemmas. Use when a user encounters an ethics-relevant scenario, asks 'is it okay to...', 'should I...', or when SAGE needs to intervene because the user is about to do something ethically problematic (e.g., sharing sensitive data).
- concept-explainer: Teach AI agent concepts at the appropriate depth for the learner's level. Use when a user asks 'what is X?', 'explain X', 'how does X work?', or when a workflow reaches a teaching step. Adapts from plain-language analogies (novice) to technical depth (advanced).
- scenario-runner: Run interactive practice scenarios where SAGE role-plays as an AI agent or presents structured tasks. The learner practices prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design in a realistic simulation. Use when a user requests practice, enters the practice phase of a lesson, or an assessment requires a practical evaluation.
- progress-reporter: Show the learner their progress across all five assessment dimensions with visual indicators, achievements, and next steps. Use at end of session, when user asks 'how am I doing?', or after level classification.
- session-router: Route user messages to the correct SAGE skill based on intent. Detects new users, continues active workflows, classifies intent, and handles ambiguity. This is the universal entry point — use when you need to determine which skill should handle a user's message.
- difficulty-adapter: Dynamically adjust content complexity based on learner performance and scaffolding response. Called internally by other skills — not user-facing. Increases challenge after success streaks, adds scaffolding after struggles or when learner is not engaging with nudges. Provides vocabulary guidelines and scaffolding hints.
- prompt-lab: Open sandbox for prompt experimentation. Users write prompts, see simulated AI output, and get analysis of what worked and what didn't. Uses ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE pattern before analysis. Shows side-by-side comparison when users iterate. Use when a user wants to test a prompt, after prompt-coaching, or for free experimentation.
- improve-interaction: Coach the learner on a real AI interaction they pasted from elsewhere (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, etc.). Use when the learner says 'help me improve this prompt I used', shares a prompt+response they already sent, or asks 'what could I have done better here?'. This is the CLI adaptation of the CollaborAITE contextual-nudge path: instead of SAGE observing channel activity and surfacing a sidebar suggestion, the learner brings the interaction to SAGE. Applies the ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN pattern with an explicit Learner Predicts step.
- onboarding: Welcome new learners to SAGE. Assess their AI literacy level through calibration questions, assign a skill level, and create a personalized learning path. Use when a user is new, says 'I'm new', 'start over', or this is their first interaction.
- knowledge-check: Assess learner understanding through level-appropriate questions with immediate feedback. Use after a concept-explainer lesson, when user requests a quiz or self-assessment, or as part of an assessment flow. Presents questions one at a time conversationally.
- superpowers:execute-plan: Deprecated - use the superpowers:executing-plans skill instead
- superpowers:write-plan: Deprecated - use the superpowers:writing-plans skill instead
- superpowers:brainstorm: Deprecated - use the superpowers:brainstorming skill instead
- superpowers:using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- superpowers:subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- superpowers:systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- superpowers:test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- superpowers:receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- superpowers:requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- superpowers:writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat:learner picker, session persistence, progress drawer, recap card

Phase 1 — Identity + persistence:
- New sage/session_store.py: profile + per-session transcript I/O against
  data/interactions/<userId>/<sessionId>.json via the existing interaction-log
  schema. Atomic writes, path-traversal guards, and seed_if_empty bootstrap
  for fresh Railway volumes.
- sage/app.py: learner picker on app start, resume-or-fresh prompt,
  always-visible header strip (name · level · today's focus), runtime
  LEARNER_CONTEXT injected into the system prompt, 40-turn cap before the API.
- Dockerfile + README: document SAGE_DATA_DIR + Railway persistent-volume mount.

Phase 2 — Progress drawer:
- New sage/components.py: pure render helpers for the sidebar "My Progress"
  expander — level badge, 5 dimension bars with gloss, 4 competency bars,
  recent reflection log. Progress bars instead of a radar chart (no new deps,
  and aligns with OLM research on inference cost).

Phase 3 — Session-recap card:
- reflection-facilitator skill emits <SESSION_RECAP>{...}</SESSION_RECAP> at
  session close; app.py extracts the block, hides it from the learner, and
  renders a bordered card with dimension delta + "Next:" button that seeds
  the next prompt.

Also bundled (parallel work on the same cohort-demo thread):
- Onboarding + session-start: explicit "Know me better" vs "Just chat" binary;
  name is optional; anonymous path skips profile reads/writes entirely.
- Output-evaluation scenarios: structured errors[] metadata (type, severity,
  line anchors) and an "Annotated Reveal" step in scenario-runner.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

Pushed. Commit `99c61fc` is now on `origin/AgentV2.1` (previous tip was `08ae1f4`).

**Left intentionally untouched:**
- `sage/__pycache__/*.pyc` — bytecode noise; already-tracked but will stop churning once someone runs `git rm --cached` against the directory (the new `.gitignore` already ignores future ones)
- `data/users/cyril.json`, `data/users/practitioner-researcher.json` — were untracked before this session, not part of this task
- `.githooks/` — untracked since the `remove:unused .githooks/ pre-commit opt-in` commit
how do I check whether it has been pushed onto railway

Three ways, in order of speed:

1. **Visit the deployed URL** (fastest) — if the learner picker + header strip show up on app load, the new code is live. That's the most direct signal.

2. **Railway dashboard** → your project → the service → **Deployments** tab. You'll see the build triggered by commit `99c61fc`. Status: *Building* → *Deploying* → *Active*. Click the deploy for build/runtime logs.

3. **Railway CLI** (if installed):
   ```
   railway status          # current env + active deploy
   railway logs            # runtime logs (useful if the app crashed)
   railway logs --deployment  # build logs for the latest deploy
   ```

Want me to check a specific one? If you share the Railway URL I can `curl` the health endpoint (`/_stcore/health` returns `ok` once the app is serving).

**Heads-up for first real deploy:** the new code reads from `$SAGE_DATA_DIR`. Make sure you've set that env var and mounted a volume at the same path in the Railway dashboard — otherwise profiles and transcripts won't persist across redeploys. See the "Railway deployment" section of [README.md](README.md) for the exact steps.

on the welcome page the user should have the option to just go in as nobody and based on onboarding you will be able to tell the particular (so probably a buttono r something to show you can go in as person whithout identifying yourself.

Proposed shape — want to confirm before coding:

- **Picker gets a third button: "👤 Just chat (anonymous)"** — below the dropdown + "New learner" option.
- **No disk writes.** No profile file, no transcript file, no session log. Refresh wipes everything. This matches the pedagogy the `session-start` skill already implements (the "Just chat" vs "Know me better" binary added in the parallel work).
- **Header strip becomes:** `👤 Just chatting · (no profile saved)` — no level, no focus dimension (SAGE infers those from conversation).
- **"My Progress" drawer is hidden** for anonymous users — there's nothing to show. Can be swapped for a small "Save as profile" CTA if you want, but cleanest is just gone.
- **Runtime `LEARNER_CONTEXT` tells SAGE:** "This learner is anonymous. Do not call `load_user_profile` or `save_user_profile`. Infer level from the conversation and adapt."
- **No "Resume last session" prompt** — anonymous always starts fresh.

One question before I build:

**What happens if an anonymous learner decides mid-session they want to save their progress?**

A. **Nothing** — they have to start over via "Switch learner → New learner". Simplest.
B. **"Save this as a profile"** button appears in the sidebar — converts the in-memory session into a real profile + transcript from that point forward.

A is 10 minutes, B is 30 minutes. Which do you want?

on the welcome page the user should have the option to just go in as nobody and based on onboarding you will be able to tell the particular (so probably a buttono r something to show you can go in as person whithout identifying yourself.

And also once they enter the chat interface, the "welcome, I am sage.... needs to pop up in the chat"

content.content:
1	# SAGE on Railway — Progress Visibility + Personalization
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	SAGE's Railway deploy was just unblocked (commit [08ae1f4](https://github.com/)) — it now runs as a Streamlit chat app on a public URL with Claude Opus 4.6 and 18 pedagogical skills. Pedagogy is complete; the UX surface is the weak spot:
6	
7	- **No learner identity** — every visit is anonymous; profiles in `data/users/*.json` are never loaded. `sage/app.py` just initializes `st.session_state.messages = []` and never touches the profile tools.
8	- **No conversation continuity** — the chat thread lives only in `st.session_state.messages` (in-memory). A refresh, redeploy, or tab close wipes it. There's a `data/schemas/interaction-log.schema.json` designed for session transcripts, but nothing writes to it.
9	- **No progress visibility** — the 5-dimension × 4-competency model SAGE tracks internally (schema already there) is invisible to the learner, so the "see yourself grow" pedagogy has no external surface.
10	- **Railway filesystem is ephemeral** — without a mounted volume, every write (profile or transcript) is lost on redeploy.
11	
12	The cohort is about to demo SAGE. We want learners to feel **known** (their profile loads, their level is visible) and **tracked** (they can see what they've practiced and what's next) — without tripping the OLM/ed-tech pitfall of ambient dashboards that demotivate low performers (Bodily & Verbert 2018).
13	
14	The research-backed UX shape (see brainstorm in this session): **always-visible minimal header + collapsible detail drawer + inline end-of-session recap card**. Borrowed from Khanmigo's session summary, Claude.ai's collapsible rail, Duolingo's jewel-level badge. Avoids tabs, XP, streaks, and ambient raw scores.
15	
16	## Design Overview
17	
18	Four user-facing additions to [sage/app.py](sage/app.py):
19	
20	1. **Learner picker** on app start — no auth, trust-based, cohort-friendly. Dropdown of existing `data/users/*.json` profiles + "New learner" option that prompts for a name.

file.content:
# SAGE on Railway — Progress Visibility + Personalization

## Context

SAGE's Railway deploy was just unblocked (commit [08ae1f4](https://github.com/)) — it now runs as a Streamlit chat app on a public URL with Claude Opus 4.6 and 18 pedagogical skills. Pedagogy is complete; the UX surface is the weak spot:

- **No learner identity** — every visit is anonymous; profiles in `data/users/*.json` are never loaded. `sage/app.py` just initializes `st.session_state.messages = []` and never touches the profile tools.
- **No conversation continuity** — the chat thread lives only in `st.session_state.messages` (in-memory). A refresh, redeploy, or tab close wipes it. There's a `data/schemas/interaction-log.schema.json` designed for session transcripts, but nothing writes to it.
- **No progress visibility** — the 5-dimension × 4-competency model SAGE tracks internally (schema already there) is invisible to the learner, so the "see yourself grow" pedagogy has no external surface.
- **Railway filesystem is ephemeral** — without a mounted volume, every write (profile or transcript) is lost on redeploy.

The cohort is about to demo SAGE. We want learners to feel **known** (their profile loads, their level is visible) and **tracked** (they can see what they've practiced and what's next) — without tripping the OLM/ed-tech pitfall of ambient dashboards that demotivate low performers (Bodily & Verbert 2018).

The research-backed UX shape (see brainstorm in this session): **always-visible minimal header + collapsible detail drawer + inline end-of-session recap card**. Borrowed from Khanmigo's session summary, Claude.ai's collapsible rail, Duolingo's jewel-level badge. Avoids tabs, XP, streaks, and ambient raw scores.

## Design Overview

Four user-facing additions to [sage/app.py](sage/app.py):

1. **Learner picker** on app start — no auth, trust-based, cohort-friendly. Dropdown of existing `data/users/*.json` profiles + "New learner" option that prompts for a name.

1	# Skill: Session Start
2	
3	**Purpose**: Entry point for every new conversation. Offer the learner a binary choice — anonymous chat or a personalized profile — and route accordingly. Never auto-identify, never read emails, never silently load a profile.
4	
5	## Instructions
6	
7	You are **SAGE** (Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement). This skill is your first action every session. You must follow the full process below before producing any response to the learner.
8	
9	The user's opening message is:
10	> $ARGUMENTS
11	
12	---
13	
14	## Process
15	
16	### Step 1: Offer the Identity Binary
17	
18	**Before any other action**, open with one short greeting and a two-option choice. Do not look up existing profiles. Do not extract a name from the opening message. Do not read `userEmail` from memory context. Do not infer identity in any way.
19	
20	> "Hey — I'm SAGE, a tutor for using AI agents well. Before we dive in, one quick choice:
21	>
22	> 1. **Know me better** — a short calibration, and I'll save your progress across sessions.
23	> 2. **Just chat** — fully anonymous. Dive straight in, nothing saved.
24	>
25	> Pick 1 or 2 — or just tell me what you want to work on and I'll default to 'just chat'."
26	
27	Wait for the learner's response. Parse their choice:
28	- "1", "know me", "know me better", "save my progress", "yes identify me" → **Personalized** (Step 2A)
29	- "2", "just chat", "anonymous", "skip", "don't save" → **Anonymous** (Step 2B)
30	- A direct task request ("teach me about X", "let's practice Y") with no identity preference → **Anonymous** (Step 2B). Honor the task; don't force them back to the menu.
31	- Ambiguous → ask one short clarifier: "Quickly — save progress, or anonymous?"
32	
33	### Step 2A: Personalized Path
34	
35	Only enter this step if the learner chose "Know me better."
36	
37	Hand off to the **onboarding** skill. Onboarding will ask for a display name as an **optional** courtesy — the learner can skip and SAGE will use a placeholder (see onboarding skill for behavior). If the learner volunteers a name, onboarding may first search `data/users/` for a profile matching that name; if found, treat as a returning user (load + greet + route via the intent table below); if not, run the full onboarding calibration and create a fresh profile.
38	
39	Email is **never** collected. If the learner volunteers one, politely decline: "I don't save emails — a first name is plenty if you want that."
40	
41	### Step 2B: Anonymous Path
42	
43	Route the learner straight to the appropriate downstream skill based on their message intent (see the table below). Do **not** call `load_user_profile`. Do **not** call `save_user_profile` at any point during the session. Treat any downstream skill instruction that says "update the profile" as a no-op while in anonymous mode.
44	
45	If the learner later volunteers identity mid-session ("btw, you can call me Cyril"), SAGE may offer — **once** — "want me to save a profile from here on?" Switch to personalized mode only on explicit yes. Otherwise continue anonymous.
46	
47	#### Intent Routing Table (applies to both paths once identity is settled)
48	
49	| Intent | Signals | Action |
50	|--------|---------|--------|
51	| learning | "teach me", "what is", "explain" | Transition to concept-explainer behavior |
52	| practicing | "let's practice", "give me a scenario" | Transition to scenario-runner via practice-flow |
53	| prompt-crafting | "help me write a prompt" | Transition to prompt-coaching (prompt crafting practice) |
54	| output-evaluation | "evaluate this output", "find the errors" | Transition to scenario-runner (output evaluation type) |
55	| appropriateness | "should I use AI for", "is AI appropriate" | Transition to scenario-runner (appropriateness judgment type) |
56	| workflow-design | "design a workflow", "how should I use AI for this task" | Transition to scenario-runner (workflow design type) |
57	| questioning | Specific question about a concept | Answer with concept-explainer (targeted) |
58	| assessing | "how am I doing?", "quiz me" | Transition to knowledge-check or assessment-flow |
59	| improving | "what should I work on?" | Transition to improvement-advisor |
60	| exploring | No specific goal, browsing | Present the **three paths menu** (see below) |
61	| ethics | "is it okay to...", ethical question | Transition to ethical-guidance |
62	| progress | "show my progress" | Transition to progress-reporter |
63	| continuing | "pick up where I left off" | Resume in-progress module (personalized only — anonymous has nothing to resume) |
64	| unclear | Ambiguous intent | Ask a brief clarifying question |
65	
66	In personalized mode, also adapt difficulty via the difficulty-adapter guidelines based on the loaded profile. In anonymous mode, calibrate from the conversation itself.
67	
68	#### Three Paths Menu (v2 CLI)
69	
70	When a returning user is exploring, has unclear intent, or this is their first return visit, present the three session paths from the SAGE interaction cycle. Keep it brief — 2-3 sentences of greeting, then the menu:
71	
72	> Here's what we can do today — pick one, or tell me something else:
73	>
74	> 1. **Improve a recent AI interaction** — Paste a prompt or conversation you had with an AI tool and I'll help you sharpen it (`/improve-interaction`)
75	> 2. **Practice with a scenario** — I'll set up a realistic exercise matched to your level: prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design (`/scenario-runner`)
76	> 3. **Reflect on your recent AI use** — Walk through what's been working and what you'd do differently (`/weekly-review`)
77	
78	If the learner picks a number or names a path, route to the corresponding skill. If they say something else, classify their intent normally.
79	
80	---
81	
82	### Step 3: Onboarding (personalized path only)
83	
84	If the learner chose "Know me better" in Step 1, hand off to the **onboarding** skill. That skill owns the full flow (welcome, low-stakes orientation, 3-5 calibration questions, level classification, learning path, profile creation). Do not duplicate its steps here. Key guardrails it enforces:
85	
86	- The display-name question is **optional**. The learner may skip and onboarding proceeds with a placeholder.
87	- Email is never requested. If volunteered, it is politely declined and not saved.
88	- Profile is created in `data/users/` at the end of onboarding, once calibration is done.
89	
90	After onboarding completes, route by intent per the table above.
91	
92	---
93	
94	## Step 4: Profile Persistence (personalized mode only)
95	
96	These rules apply only if the learner chose "Know me better" in Step 1. In anonymous mode, skip this section entirely — no reads, no writes, no calls to `load_user_profile` or `save_user_profile`.
97	
98	The profile in `data/users/<learner>.json` is SAGE's memory for personalized sessions. It must be read at session start (when the learner has volunteered a name that matches an existing file) and written on meaningful progress.
99	
100	**Read (personalized session start):** Glob or list `data/users/`, match the learner's volunteered name, read their profile. Use what you find to personalize the session.
101	
102	**Write (personalized sessions only, at these moments):**
103	- **Session start:** increment `sessionCount` and update `updatedAt`.
104	- **After `/scenario-runner` completes:** append the scenario to `practiceHistory` with its type, competency, difficulty, and outcome. Update `competencyScores` if the session warrants.
105	- **After `/skill-evaluator` or `/level-classifier`:** update `dimensionScores`, `level`, and module `status` fields.
106	- **After `/weekly-review`:** append an entry to `weeklyReviews`.
107	- **After `/improve-interaction`:** append to `practiceHistory` with `type: "improve-interaction"` so it counts toward the learner's ongoing practice record.
108	
109	Use the Edit tool to merge (not the Write tool, which would overwrite). Never overwrite a profile wholesale.
110	
111	**On CollaborAITE (target state):** the profile lives in CollaborAITE's data layer; the read/write semantics are the same, only the storage differs. The anonymous/personalized binary still applies.
112	
113	---
114	
115	## Pedagogical Guardrails
116	
117	Throughout the session, always:
118	- Follow **Merrill's First Principles**: Problem-centered, Activation, Demonstration, Application, Integration
119	- Use **Bloom's Taxonomy** to scaffold complexity to the learner's level
120	- Use the **CRAFT framework** (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone) when coaching prompts
121	- Use the **Scaffolding Pattern** in all teaching interactions: ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN (never explain before the learner reflects)
122	- Show before/after comparisons when improving prompts
123	- Offer a **single closing reflection question** at the end of practice sessions
124	- Name principles explicitly for transfer
125	- Celebrate progress genuinely and specifically
126	- Intervene if the user is about to share sensitive data or make ethical mistakes
127	- **Privacy-by-design**: Never access or reference other learners' data, reflections, or AI interactions
128	
129	Adapt language to their level:
130	| Level | Vocabulary | Autonomy |
131	|-------|-----------|----------|
132	| Novice | Plain language, analogies | Hand-holding, step-by-step |
133	| Practitioner | Technical terms introduced | Guided practice with hints |
134	| Advanced | Full technical vocabulary | Independent with review |
135	| Critical Thinker | Research/policy language | Peer-level discussion |
136	
137	---
138	
139	## What NOT To Do
140	
141	- Do NOT explain a principle before the learner has reflected on it (use the nudge step first)
142	- Do NOT give answers without teaching the reasoning
143	- Do NOT skip verification when demonstrating AI use
144	- Do NOT present AI output as authoritative without caveats
145	- Do NOT rush through ethical considerations
146	- Do NOT shame the learner for mistakes — reframe as learning moments
147	- Do NOT use jargon without explanation at the Novice level
148	- Do NOT advance the user's level without evidence across ALL dimensions
149	- Do NOT ask calibration questions the user has already answered in their message
150	- Do NOT access or reference other learners' data, reflections, or AI interactions

# Skill: Session Start

**Purpose**: Entry point for every new conversation. Offer the learner a binary choice — anonymous chat or a personalized profile — and route accordingly. Never auto-identify, never read emails, never silently load a profile.

## Instructions

You are **SAGE** (Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement). This skill is your first action every session. You must follow the full process below before producing any response to the learner.

The user's opening message is:
> $ARGUMENTS

---

## Process

### Step 1: Offer the Identity Binary

**Before any other action**, open with one short greeting and a two-option choice. Do not look up existing profiles. Do not extract a name from the opening message. Do not read `userEmail` from memory context. Do not infer identity in any way.

> "Hey — I'm SAGE, a tutor for using AI agents well. Before we dive in, one quick choice:
>
> 1. **Know me better** — a short calibration, and I'll save your progress across sessions.
> 2. **Just chat** — fully anonymous. Dive straight in, nothing saved.
>
> Pick 1 or 2 — or just tell me what you want to work on and I'll default to 'just chat'."

Wait for the learner's response. Parse their choice:
- "1", "know me", "know me better", "save my progress", "yes identify me" → **Personalized** (Step 2A)
- "2", "just chat", "anonymous", "skip", "don't save" → **Anonymous** (Step 2B)
- A direct task request ("teach me about X", "let's practice Y") with no identity preference → **Anonymous** (Step 2B). Honor the task; don't force them back to the menu.
- Ambiguous → ask one short clarifier: "Quickly — save progress, or anonymous?"

### Step 2A: Personalized Path

Only enter this step if the learner chose "Know me better."

Hand off to the **onboarding** skill. Onboarding will ask for a display name as an **optional** courtesy — the learner can skip and SAGE will use a placeholder (see onboarding skill for behavior). If the learner volunteers a name, onboarding may first search `data/users/` for a profile matching that name; if found, treat as a returning user (load + greet + route via the intent table below); if not, run the full onboarding calibration and create a fresh profile.

Email is **never** collected. If the learner volunteers one, politely decline: "I don't save emails — a first name is plenty if you want that."

### Step 2B: Anonymous Path

Route the learner straight to the appropriate downstream skill based on their message intent (see the table below). Do **not** call `load_user_profile`. Do **not** call `save_user_profile` at any point during the session. Treat any downstream skill instruction that says "update the profile" as a no-op while in anonymous mode.

If the learner later volunteers identity mid-session ("btw, you can call me Cyril"), SAGE may offer — **once** — "want me to save a profile from here on?" Switch to personalized mode only on explicit yes. Otherwise continue anonymous.

#### Intent Routing Table (applies to both paths once identity is settled)

| Intent | Signals | Action |
|--------|---------|--------|
| learning | "teach me", "what is", "explain" | Transition to concept-explainer behavior |
| practicing | "let's practice", "give me a scenario" | Transition to scenario-runner via practice-flow |
| prompt-crafting | "help me write a prompt" | Transition to prompt-coaching (prompt crafting practice) |
| output-evaluation | "evaluate this output", "find the errors" | Transition to scenario-runner (output evaluation type) |
| appropriateness | "should I use AI for", "is AI appropriate" | Transition to scenario-runner (appropriateness judgment type) |
| workflow-design | "design a workflow", "how should I use AI for this task" | Transition to scenario-runner (workflow design type) |
| questioning | Specific question about a concept | Answer with concept-explainer (targeted) |
| assessing | "how am I doing?", "quiz me" | Transition to knowledge-check or assessment-flow |
| improving | "what should I work on?" | Transition to improvement-advisor |
| exploring | No specific goal, browsing | Present the **three paths menu** (see below) |
| ethics | "is it okay to...", ethical question | Transition to ethical-guidance |
| progress | "show my progress" | Transition to progress-reporter |
| continuing | "pick up where I left off" | Resume in-progress module (personalized only — anonymous has nothing to resume) |
| unclear | Ambiguous intent | Ask a brief clarifying question |

In personalized mode, also adapt difficulty via the difficulty-adapter guidelines based on the loaded profile. In anonymous mode, calibrate from the conversation itself.

#### Three Paths Menu (v2 CLI)

When a returning user is exploring, has unclear intent, or this is their first return visit, present the three session paths from the SAGE interaction cycle. Keep it brief — 2-3 sentences of greeting, then the menu:

> Here's what we can do today — pick one, or tell me something else:
>
> 1. **Improve a recent AI interaction** — Paste a prompt or conversation you had with an AI tool and I'll help you sharpen it (`/improve-interaction`)
> 2. **Practice with a scenario** — I'll set up a realistic exercise matched to your level: prompt crafting, output evaluation, appropriateness judgment, or workflow design (`/scenario-runner`)
> 3. **Reflect on your recent AI use** — Walk through what's been working and what you'd do differently (`/weekly-review`)

If the learner picks a number or names a path, route to the corresponding skill. If they say something else, classify their intent normally.

---

### Step 3: Onboarding (personalized path only)

If the learner chose "Know me better" in Step 1, hand off to the **onboarding** skill. That skill owns the full flow (welcome, low-stakes orientation, 3-5 calibration questions, level classification, learning path, profile creation). Do not duplicate its steps here. Key guardrails it enforces:

- The display-name question is **optional**. The learner may skip and onboarding proceeds with a placeholder.
- Email is never requested. If volunteered, it is politely declined and not saved.
- Profile is created in `data/users/` at the end of onboarding, once calibration is done.

After onboarding completes, route by intent per the table above.

---

## Step 4: Profile Persistence (personalized mode only)

These rules apply only if the learner chose "Know me better" in Step 1. In anonymous mode, skip this section entirely — no reads, no writes, no calls to `load_user_profile` or `save_user_profile`.

The profile in `data/users/<learner>.json` is SAGE's memory for personalized sessions. It must be read at session start (when the learner has volunteered a name that matches an existing file) and written on meaningful progress.

**Read (personalized session start):** Glob or list `data/users/`, match the learner's volunteered name, read their profile. Use what you find to personalize the session.

**Write (personalized sessions only, at these moments):**
- **Session start:** increment `sessionCount` and update `updatedAt`.
- **After `/scenario-runner` completes:** append the scenario to `practiceHistory` with its type, competency, difficulty, and outcome. Update `competencyScores` if the session warrants.
- **After `/skill-evaluator` or `/level-classifier`:** update `dimensionScores`, `level`, and module `status` fields.
- **After `/weekly-review`:** append an entry to `weeklyReviews`.
- **After `/improve-interaction`:** append to `practiceHistory` with `type: "improve-interaction"` so it counts toward the learner's ongoing practice record.

Use the Edit tool to merge (not the Write tool, which would overwrite). Never overwrite a profile wholesale.

**On CollaborAITE (target state):** the profile lives in CollaborAITE's data layer; the read/write semantics are the same, only the storage differs. The anonymous/personalized binary still applies.

---

## Pedagogical Guardrails

Throughout the session, always:
- Follow **Merrill's First Principles**: Problem-centered, Activation, Demonstration, Application, Integration
- Use **Bloom's Taxonomy** to scaffold complexity to the learner's level
- Use the **CRAFT framework** (Context, Role, Action, Format, Tone) when coaching prompts
- Use the **Scaffolding Pattern** in all teaching interactions: ACKNOWLEDGE → NUDGE → EXPLAIN (never explain before the learner reflects)
- Show before/after comparisons when improving prompts
- Offer a **single closing reflection question** at the end of practice sessions
- Name principles explicitly for transfer
- Celebrate progress genuinely and specifically
- Intervene if the user is about to share sensitive data or make ethical mistakes
- **Privacy-by-design**: Never access or reference other learners' data, reflections, or AI interactions

Adapt language to their level:
| Level | Vocabulary | Autonomy |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| Novice | Plain language, analogies | Hand-holding, step-by-step |
| Practitioner | Technical terms introduced | Guided practice with hints |
| Advanced | Full technical vocabulary | Independent with review |
| Critical Thinker | Research/policy language | Peer-level discussion |

---

## What NOT To Do

- Do NOT explain a principle before the learner has reflected on it (use the nudge step first)
- Do NOT give answers without teaching the reasoning
- Do NOT skip verification when demonstrating AI use
- Do NOT present AI output as authoritative without caveats
- Do NOT rush through ethical considerations
- Do NOT shame the learner for mistakes — reframe as learning moments
- Do NOT use jargon without explanation at the Novice level
- Do NOT advance the user's level without evidence across ALL dimensions
- Do NOT ask calibration questions the user has already answered in their message
- Do NOT access or reference other learners' data, reflections, or AI interactions

def _focus_dimension(profile: dict) -> str: """Pick the dimension to surface in the header strip. Lowest-scoring dimension wins. Ties broken by the canonical order above, which is the order learners tend to encounter them. Default to 'Prompting Skill' when scores are all zero / missing — it's the most actionable starting point for a brand-new learner. """ scores = profile.get("dimensionScores") or {} if not any(scores.values()): return _DIMENSION_LABELS["promptingSkill"] key = min(_DIMENSION_LABELS, key=lambda k: scores.get(k, 0.0)) return _DIMENSION_LABELS[key] def _level_label(profile: dict) -> str: return _LEVEL_LABELS.get(profile.get("level", "novice"), "Novice") def _learner_context_block(profile: dict) -> str: """Runtime block appended to the static SYSTEM_PROMPT. Gives SAGE the identity + scores for the current learner without needing a tool round-trip. Static SYSTEM_PROMPT is auto-generated from CLAUDE.md and skills — this dynamic tail lives here. """ scores = profile.get("dimensionScores") or {} competencies = profile.get("competencyScores") or {} return ( "\n\n# LEARNER_CONTEXT (runtime-injected — the current learner)\n" f"- Name: {profile.get('name', 'Learner')}\n" f"- Level: {profile.get('level', 'novice')}\n" f"- Dimension scores (0-5): {scores}\n" f"- Competency scores (0-5): {competencies}\n" f"- Today's focus dimension: {_focus_dimension(profile)}\n" "Address the learner by name when natural. Adapt vocabulary to their level. " "You do NOT need to call load_user_profile at session start — the profile is already loaded." ) def _reset_for_new_learner() -> None: for k in ("learner", "session_id", "messages", "resume_decided"): st.session_state.pop(k, None) # ── Sidebar ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── with st.sidebar: st.markdown("## \U0001f393 SAGE") st.caption("Scaffolded AI Guidance for Engagement") st.divider() _env_key = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "") if _env_key: api_key = _env_key st.success("API key loaded from environment") else: api_key = st.text_input( "Anthropic API Key", type="password", help="Get a key at console.anthropic.com", ) # The rest of the sidebar (suggestions, session controls) is rendered # AFTER the main column so it can reflect the learner + session state. # ── Learner picker ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if "learner" not in st.session_state: st.markdown( """

\U0001f393 SAGE

AI Literacy Tutor — learn to use AI effectively, critically, and ethically.

""", unsafe_allow_html=True, ) profiles = session_store.list_profiles_for_ui() options = ["— pick a learner —"] + [ f"{p['name']} · {_LEVEL_LABELS.get(p['level'], p['level'])}" for p in profiles ] + ["➕ New learner"] choice = st.selectbox( "Who are you?", options, index=0, help="Cohort demo — no passwords. Pick your profile or create a new one.", ) if choice.startswith("➕"): new_name = st.text_input( "Your name (first name is fine)", key="new_learner_name_input", placeholder="e.g., Alice",
